text: stop it. Having to wait through that…it was the worst time of my life. It was agonizing, feeling so helpless .” Helpless . It seemed an adequate word. For the first time in one hundred and eighty-five years, Ronin felt that, no matter what choice he made, no matter what actions he did or did not take, the outcome would be wholly unaffected. Lara would live or die, and he had no power over it . “This is normal?” Ronin asked finally . Will chuckled, turning away to power on a monitor and lift a small tool from a nearby tray. A camera on the end of the tool relayed Ronin’s internal damage to the screen . “Helplessness is part of the everyday human condition .” “I’m not human .” “Why? Because you’re made up of different parts ?” On the monitor, neat bundles of wiring and circuitry ran along segmented metal columns, all around the central box that housed Ronin’s CPU and data cores . “Those parts are just one thing on a long list of differences .” “Yes, in some ways. But an organic lifeform and a machine are not entirely different. Hell, if anyone knows, it’d be me.” Will guided the camera along the scrapes and grooves created by the bullets’ passage. “I’m the sixth generation of a family that’s devoted its entire life to robotics. The sixth William Anderson. You think you have identity issues?” He laughed again, it was a warm sound . The closest Ronin had heard was from Lara. Genuine laughter was a rare thing, it seemed, but it had a strange way of alleviating tension . “You’re not your father, or your grandfather, or any of them,” Ronin said . “Right. But I’m expected to be.” Will fell silent for a moment. “An inch off the trajectory of any of these, and you might’ve been in some serious trouble. These armor-piercing rounds they used everywhere during the war can chew through inner casings like they’re made of paper, at close range .” “Better me than her .” “I don’t think my great-great-great grandfather could’ve foreseen any of this, but I bet he would’ve been delighted over it .” “Over what?” Ronin asked. “Over what happened to the world ?” “No. He abhorred that. I mean the way bots have evolved. His work, when you get down to it, was the very basics of how you operate now. Everything in the time since…you’ve developed it on your own. You have a life . In the old world, just the thought of that was enough to cause widespread panic .” Will selected a few more tools, watching the monitor as he slipped them into the bullet hole and began the repairs. Sparks danced across Ronin’s electrodes, but only distantly . “Does that mean the war started because of bots ?” “I don’t think so. To be honest, it’s hard to say why it happened anymore, even with the records we have. I think it was building for a long time. Bots might’ve been what tipped it, but that was an excuse more than anything. The whole point is that you have free will. You can experience emotions, you have doubts and regrets, you can fall in love. That is what it means to be human. It was never about being an organic bipedal lifeform evolved from primates .” Ronin lay in silence, ignoring the vibrations pulsing through his insides as Will worked. It couldn’t be that simple, could it? He’d wandered for decades, only experiencing the merest hints of emotion, never knowing how strong they could be until Lara . Had he always possessed the capability for deeper feeling ? Perhaps the answer was simple — he’d always been capable, but it had taken Lara to give him something to care for. She’d sparked his emotions, and now they burned bright. She was the catalyst of his evolution . “You also have free will,” Ronin said. “You’re no more defined by your name than I am my parts .” “You’re right,” Will replied, smiling, “but I spent a lot of time worrying about it, anyway. It’s a lot to live up to. A family legacy that can easily eclipse anything I might do. What’s important, though, is that we’ve preserved his vision in this place. Bots and humans coexisting, having relationships, living side-by-side. I can’t deny that I’m proud to be a part of that .” “I owe much to you and your ancestors, Will Anderson .” Will laughed again. “Don’t get me wrong — I was relieved as hell when I found out we were having a girl. It broke the six-generation-chain of sons. Meant I didn’t have to make some excuse to explain to dad why I wasn’t going to name my kid William Joseph Anderson the Seventh .” Those generations had come and gone while Ronin walked the Dust. He’d been aware of the cycle of human life, but never considered it more than superficially. So many humans had joined together, built families and raised children, only for the process to repeat . “Newton told me there were humans and bots who came together to form families,” Ronin said . “Yeah. Nobody’s sure how many there were, before. But there’s about a dozen here on the base. One more, I guess, with you two here .” “Do they...adopt children?” Why was he concerned about it? Was this the result of raw emotion, or innocent curiosity ? “Not many orphans running around here, usually. There’s a way for male synths to reproduce, as long as there’s a donor. Not very complicated, really, and we’ve done it a few times over the years .” Ronin nodded, turning his head away from the monitor as Will continued the repairs. Would Lara be happy to learn that news? Would she embrace the idea, or reject it outright ? Did he accept it? Before Lara, love had been an airy, ethereal word with little meaning to
